Who should watch out 'round the corner? Setting Down the Spelling Rules Some rules make life hard for your child. She wants to feel the soft grass under her toes, but the sign says to keep out, or she wants to bomb into the blue water, but the lifeguard warns her against any kind of jumping. Not so with spelling rules. Spelling rules help a great deal as long as you don't overload your child with them. Three or four rules cover pretty much all the important spelling factors that your child needs to know. Rule One: Bossy e You write Bossy e everywhere. That's why it's a great letter for your child to get to know. Here's the rule: Your child spells a word like mate by adding Bossy e to the end of mat. The e is bossy because it bosses the other vowel in the word to make a long sound or to shout out its name. Here are a few words that show Bossy e doing its thing: plate, mate, lake, Pete, scene, ride, hide, mine, rode, bone, hope, cute, mule and tune. When a vowel makes a long sound, it's the same sound as its name. Bossy e makes the vowel (in the word it tags onto) have a long sound or shout out its name. Rule Two: When two vowels go walking, the first one does the talking Here's a cute rhyme to help your child remember another way of spelling long vowel sounds. When spelling words like neat and boat, your child can go over the when-two-vowels-go-walking-the-first-one-does-the-talking rhyme. Most long-e sounds fit the two vowels rule. Long e is spelled either with ee, like in meet, seed, and weed, or ea, like in team, seat, and bead. The two vowels come side by side, but the first is the one that makes its long sound. A long-o sound is also often spelled with two vowels. The long o is spelled with oa like in boat, coat, and loan. A long-a sound can be spelled with side-by-side vowels, too. In words like pain and rain, your child spells the long-a sound with ai. See how the two vowels come together? Your child needs to start them with the long vowel that he hears and then remember the partnership. Rule Three: y behaves like a vowel Without a doubt, vowel sounds are tricky to spell. That's why these four spelling rules are about them. Every time your child hears a long-vowel sound, he must run through the options, which explains why he always needs to use scrap paper when trying to spell them. The third option mostly has to do with long-e or long-i sounds that your child hears on the ends of words. The y-behaving-as-a-vowel rule applies to vowel sounds on the end of words that are spelled with a y. In words like happy and sunny, your child uses y to sound like long e. In little words like by and shy, he uses it to sound like long i. What about words like system, cyst, and gypsy? In those words, your child spells the short-i sound with a y. The words cyst and gypsy are soft-c and soft-g spellings (as well as spellings that use y to make the short-i sound) so. You use y to make e or i sounds. When your child puts y on the ends of longer words (like happy), it makes a long-e sound, and when she puts y on the end of short words (like by), it makes a long-i sound. She uses y in the middle of some words (like gypsy) to make the short-i sound. When your child gets into the habit of jotting down her spelling options on scrap paper, she gets better and better at deciding whether to use y. Rule Four: i before e except after c (when you hear ee) All sorts of words have the long-e sound in them, and the i-before-e-except-after-c rule gives your child a fourth spelling option. The question becomes: Should your child write Bossy e like in Pete, or two vowels walking like in meat and meet, y as in happy, or ie as in niece? Whew. Where is that scrap paper? With ie spellings, be sure to help your child learn the first part of the rule - i before e except after c - before helping her discover the except-when-you-hear-"ay"-like-in-neighbor part. Practice on words like niece, piece, and receive, and after she's at ease with those, tackle the tough words like neighbor and weight. (You hear ay, so you don't put i before e.) Title: How to Flirt with a Man Introduction: Flirting is a playful and fun way of expressing your interest in someone. It can be an exciting dance of attraction that involves body language, verbal communication, and emotional intelligence. This guide will provide you with detailed steps on how to effectively flirt with a man while ensuring clarity, practicality, and respect for his feelings and personal space. Step 1: Show Genuine Interest Explanation: Demonstrate that you are genuinely interested in getting to know him by asking open-ended questions about his life, opinions, hobbies, or experiences. By doing so, he'll feel valued and appreciated. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Listen attentively when he speaks * Ask thoughtful follow-up questions based on what he shares * Avoid yes/no questions as they limit conversation flow Step 2: Maintain Eye Contact Explanation: Making eye contact shows confidence and sincerity. It also creates a connection between two people, making him more likely to perceive you positively. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Look directly into his eyes when speaking and listening * Blink normally and avoid staring intensely which may come off as aggressive * Break eye contact occasionally to maintain comfort levels Step 3: Use Playful Banter Explanation: Lighthearted teasing and joking around can create a relaxed atmosphere and showcase your sense of humor. However, ensure it remains good-natured and respectful. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Tease gently without crossing boundaries or causing discomfort * Laugh at his jokes to encourage further interaction * Keep conversations light and enjoyable Step 4: Compliment Him Sincerely Explanation: A genuine compliment can make anyone feel special and boost their self-esteem. Be specific about why you admire him. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Focus on aspects unique to him such as talents, personality traits, or achievements * Deliver compliments naturally and authentically * Refrain from excessive flattery Step 5: Touch Appropriately Explanation: Physical touch, even if minor, can help build intimacy and rapport. Always obtain consent before touching and keep touches brief and casual. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Touch his arm briefly during conversation or lightly tap his shoulder when laughing at his joke * Respect his personal space and never force unwanted physical contact * Observe his reactions; if he seems uncomfortable, refrain from initiating further physical interactions Step 6: Display Confidence Explanation: Confidence attracts. Stand tall, speak clearly, and believe in yourself. Men find confident women attractive because it signals strength and independence. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Practice positive self-talk prior to approaching him * Speak assertively but kindly * Smile often to appear approachable and friendly Remember, every individual is different. Therefore, adjust these strategies according to the situation and person. Most importantly, have fun! The goal of flirting should always be enjoying the moment and building connections. Title: How to Check Startup Programs on Your Computer Introduction: Starting up your computer can take some time, especially if there are many programs set to launch automatically when you turn on your device. Some of these startup programs may be necessary for your daily tasks, while others might just be slowing down your system without providing any real benefit. This tutorial will guide you through checking your startup programs and deciding which ones to keep or remove. By following these steps, you'll ensure that your computer starts faster and runs more efficiently. **Step 1:** **Access Task Manager** Press `Ctrl + Shift + Esc` simultaneously on Windows or `Command + Space + Option` on macOS to open the Task Manager (Windows) or Activity Monitor (macOS). These utilities allow you to view currently running processes and manage them accordingly. *Key tip:* If you prefer using a graphical user interface (GUI), right-click an empty space on the taskbar (Windows) or press `Control + F2` (macOS) and type "Activity Monitor" into Spotlight Search to access the utility directly. **Step 2:** **Navigate to the Startup Tab (Windows)** In the Task Manager window, click on the 'Startup' tab at the top (if available). The 'Startup' tab shows all the applications configured to start with your operating system. It displays information such as the name of the application, its current status, and its impact on system startup. ![Task Manager](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Check-Your-Computer%27s-Startup-Programs-Step-3.jpg/aid485069-v4- You're going to divide things with him. He asked, "Mom, can I go play with my chain outside?" His mom said, "Yes, Tom, but be careful. Don't lose your chain." Tom was happy and ran outside to play. He swung his chain around and around. But because Tom was clumsy, the chain flew out of his hand and into a big tree. Tom was sad and had another question for his mom. "Mom, can you help me get my chain from the tree?" His mom smiled and said, "Yes, Tom. Let's get a long stick and try to get it down." They found a long stick and used it to get the chain back. Tom was happy again and said, "Thank you, Mom!" [CHI plays by herself with airport Mom and SIB come back] How Do We Study the Penguins? This is a device which identifies and weighs the parents as they move from the nest to the ocean to feed, and back. An electronic identification tag (like the ones in pets and race horses) has been inserted under their skin. When they go through the gate, the sensors read the tag and identifies the bird. With two sensors we can tell if the bird came in or went out. There is also a step bridge which records the weight of the bird. This is how we know how many days the birds are on the nest or out feeding, and how much they bring in to feed their chicks. The parents change places on the nest every one to three days. The chicks will grow from less than 100 grams at birth to over 3000 grams(30 times their birth weight) in about 43 days. In addition to their own food demands, parents must bring back enough food for their chicks to sustain the growth rate. Title: How to Call Cuba from Canada Introduction: Calling Cuba from Canada can be a straightforward process once you understand the steps involved. This tutorial will guide you through the entire procedure with clear, detailed instructions. By following these steps, you'll be able to make successful calls to Cuba without any hassle. Step 1: Check Your Phone Compatibility Before making a call, ensure your phone is compatible with Cuban networks. GSM phones are generally compatible, but it's always good to double-check with your service provider. If you have an older model or a CDMA phone, it might not work in Cuba. Key Tip: Unlocked phones offer more flexibility as they allow you to switch SIM cards when traveling abroad. Step 2: Know the Exact Number Format To dial correctly, know the exact number format for international calls from Canada to Cuba: 011 (Canada exit code) + 53 (Cuba country code) + Area Code + Local Number Guideline: The area codes in Cuba range from 040 to 049 followed by six digits local numbers. For instance, if you want to reach a Havana landline (area code 7), the full number would look like this: 011-53-7-XXXXXXX. Step 3: Dial the Canadian Exit Code (011) The first step when calling internationally is to dial your home country's exit code, which in this case is 011. It informs your carrier that you're making an international call. Step 4: Enter Cuba's Country Code (53) Next, input Cuba's country code - 53. Every nation has its unique country code used for global communication purposes. Step 5: Add the Recipient's Area Code and Phone Number Now, enter the recipient's area code followed by their actual telephone number. Remember, no spaces or dashes should separate these digits. Step 6: Press the Call Button After entering all the necessary information, press the 'call' button on your device. Wait while the connection is established. Step 7: Understand Potential Challenges Despite adhering to the above steps, sometimes issues may arise due to factors such as network congestion, poor signal strength, etc. Be patient; try again later if faced with such problems. Step 8: Consider Alternatives If regular calls prove challenging, consider alternatives like VoIP services (like Skype), WhatsApp calls, or email. These methods often provide reliable connections even where traditional telephony struggles. Conclusion: With modern technology, connecting with loved ones thousands of miles away is easier than ever before. While challenges persist, understanding proper procedures and utilizing alternative means ensures consistent connectivity. Whether using conventional methods or innovative apps, keep communicating, stay connected! Air is a tricky subject to teach to all children regardless of age but this experiment will begin to teach children that air is not only around us but it can also be manipulated depending on the structure. Blocked Air Air Travel Air Movement Air Currents Air Flow One strip of paper that measures 1in by 3in (2.5cm by 7.6cm) Small square box Large square box One 1-liter bottle One 2-liter bottle One Cylinder (optional) 1. Introduce the materials, preferably after you have read one of the books that we recommended. 2. Discuss the materials and ask them to make predictions on how you will be using the materials and what they feel the outcome of the experiment will be. 3. Set up the strip of paper on a table and tape one end down to the table (the one that is furthest from you.) 4. Ask one child to blow on the paper. What happens? The paper should flutter. 5. Now ask the children to predict what will happen if you place an item in front of the paper. 6. Set up a box or a bottle and have a child blow on the object (not on the paper). 7. Talk about what happens. With the curved objects, the wind will move around the curved surface and the paper will flutter. 8. Repeat with the different objects. Questions to ask: What do you think will happen to the paper if we blow on it? Why do you think the paper moved when we blew on it? What do you think will happen if we place a box in front of the paper? What do you think will happen if we place a bottle in front of the paper? Why do you think the paper fluttered with the bottle but not the box? Do you think the paper will flutter with a bigger bottle? Things to do: Document predictions that the children make and the final outcome of the experiment. Have the children document the experiment themselves with drawings or models. Show them how to use arrows to show air currents. Although this experiment is fairly straight forward, you can expand on the experiment by having the children place different objects in front of the paper to see if it is just curved objects that separates the air. You could also attach a curved object to a table and place a strong fan in front of it. Have the children place their hands on the sides of the curved object to feel the air currents running along the bottle. Try the same experiment with a large box. Do they feel wind on the sides and back of the box? Lastly, try using the airzooka, from the first experiment to determine if it changes the air flow. [housemate calls again; tape OFF/ON at 1341; 3 sec, another loud squeaking sound, sounds like a toy] Title: How to Dress for an Art Opening Art openings are exciting events that allow you to mingle with like-minded individuals, appreciate beautiful artwork, and engage in stimulating conversations. However, choosing what to wear can sometimes be a challenge. This guide will help you dress appropriately while still expressing your personal style. **Step 1: Understand the Venue and Theme** Before deciding on your outfit, research the venue and any potential themes associated with the art opening. If it's being held at a formal gallery, business casual or cocktail attire might be most appropriate. For more avant-garde exhibitions, there may not be a strict dress code, giving you room to experiment with bolder fashion choices. **Step 2: Consider Comfort** You'll likely spend several hours standing and moving around during an art opening. Therefore, choose comfortable shoes (preferably ones you can stand in for extended periods) and clothes that won't restrict your movement. Avoid outfits that require constant adjustment as they could become distracting or even annoying. **Step 3: Opt for Neutral Tones** Neutral colors such as black, white, gray, navy blue, and brown tend to work well for these types of events because they don't compete with the artwork. They also exude elegance and sophistication. However, if the event has a specific theme encouraging vibrant colors, feel free to incorporate them into your ensemble. **Step 4: Accessorize Mindfully** Accessories can elevate an outfit but should never overshadow the main attraction - the art. Keep jewelry minimal yet impactful. A pair of statement earrings, a simple bracelet, or a tasteful neckpiece can add interest without overwhelming. Similarly, bags should be small and chic; large handbags may get in the way when trying to view artwork. **Step 5: Layer Up** Layering adds versatility and visual appeal to your outfit. It allows you to adapt to changing temperatures throughout the evening and offers opportunities to showcase different aspects of your style. Scarves, cardigans, blazers, and vests are excellent layering options. **Step 6: Prioritize Quality Over Quantity** Invest in high-quality pieces made from durable materials. Not only do they last longer, but they also look better and contribute significantly to creating a polished appearance. Remember, less is often more when it comes to dressing for art openings. **Key Tips:** - **Avoid strong perfumes**: Some people might have allergies, so refrain from wearing heavy scents that could cause discomfort. - **Check the weather forecast**: Plan accordingly if the event is outdoors or if travel involves walking between venues. - **Stick to fabrics that breathe**: Natural fibers like cotton, silk, linen, and wool are breathable and comfortable. - **Consider professional grooming**: A fresh haircut, manicure, or beard trim can enhance your overall appearance. - **Test drive your outfit**: Try everything on beforehand to ensure nothing needs altering or replacing at the last minute. By following these steps and keeping our key tips in mind, you're sure to make a great impression at any art opening. Most importantly, remember that confidence is the best accessory. So, embrace your unique style and enjoy the night! I think I can get 'em by myself now. Wan' 'a see it's easy to put together. He stayed inside for more than two weeks. However, it's important to note that unauthorized copying or distribution of copyrighted material is illegal and unethical. This tutorial will focus on legally obtaining and creating copies of PC games you have already purchased for personal use only. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Original PC game disc(s) or digital download files 2. A computer with a CD/DVD drive (if using physical discs) 3. External storage device or additional internal hard drive space 4. Make sure you have enough disk space before proceeding. *Key Tip:* Keep track of where you store these downloaded files so they are easy to find later. **Step 2: Back Up Physical Discs** Launch your preferred disc-burning software. Insert the original game disc into your computer's CD/DVD drive. Choose 'Create Data Disc' or similar option within the software. Select the disc icon representing your CD/DVD drive when prompted for source files. Then choose a destination folder on your computer where the ISO image will be saved. Name the ISO accordingly for identification purposes. Finally, start the burn process. *Key Guideline:* Always ensure that you have permission to create a backup copy under local laws. Some regions allow private reproduction while others do not. **Step 3: Mount The ISO Image** After burning the ISO image, you need to mount it to make it appear like a real DVD in your system. Title: How to Change the Tweet Alert Sound on an iPhone Introduction: In this extensive guide, we will walk you through the process of changing the tweet alert sound on your iPhone. This customization allows you to personalize your device experience by selecting a unique tone that distinguishes new tweets from other alerts. We'll provide clear instructions along with key tips and guidelines to help you complete every step successfully. Let's dive into the world of iPhone settings! Step 1: Access Settings App The first step is to locate and open the "Settings" app on your iPhone's home screen or app library. The Settings app serves as the central hub where users can manage various aspects of their devices such as sounds, display, privacy, and more. By accessing this application, you are initiating the configuration required to change the tweet alert sound. Step 2: Navigate to Sounds & Haptics Within the Settings menu, scroll down until you find the option labeled "Sounds & Haptics." Tap on it to proceed. This section manages all system-generated audio cues and vibration patterns associated with different events like incoming messages, emails, calls, and social media updates (including tweets). Here, we focus specifically on altering the sound assigned to tweet notifications. Step 3: Select Social Notification Once inside the "Sounds & Haptics" tab, look for the subheading named "Sounds" followed by several clickable options representing diverse notification categories. Scroll down until you see "Twitter" listed under the "SOCIAL" category. Tapping on Twitter will allow you to modify its corresponding alert tone. If you cannot find Twitter listed directly, try locating it within the "Other" category instead. Key Tip: If you have multiple Twitter accounts linked to your device, ensure that you select the correct one before continuing. Step 4: Choose New Alert Tone After tapping on Twitter, you will be presented with two default options - "Default" and "Vibration." For our purpose, concentrate on the "Sound" field displayed above these choices. Pressing on the arrow next to it reveals a list of preinstalled ringtones categorized by genre or mood. Browse through them until you discover a sound that appeals to you. You may also opt to utilize a custom recording if previously set up via iTunes or another compatible music platform. Guideline: Remember that any changes made here will affect all linked Twitter profiles on your device unless adjusted individually per account. Step 5: Preview Selected Tone Having chosen a preferred alert tone, make sure to preview it by pressing the play button adjacent to each selection. Listening carefully enables accurate judgment regarding suitability and distinguishability among other auditory signals. It ensures satisfactory user experience and prevents potential confusion between similar-sounding alerts. Step 6: Confirm Selection & Exit Upon finalizing your choice, press the back button located at the top left corner of the screen to save the adjustments and return to the previous menu. Once back at the main "Sounds & Haptics" page, exit the app entirely either by using the home indicator at the bottom of the screen or swiping upwards from the gesture bar (for models without a physical home button). Conclusion: Congratulations! With these steps completed, you have now effectively changed the tweet alert sound on your iPhone. Personalizing your device enhances usability and satisfaction while making your digital interactions uniquely yours. Title: How to Find the Distance Between Two Points Introduction: In geometry, finding the distance between two points is a fundamental skill. Whether you're studying mathematics, engineering, or physics, this basic concept forms the foundation for many complex calculations. This tutorial will guide you through the process of calculating the distance between two points using the Euclidean formula, with clear explanations and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Understand the Problem Statement Before starting any calculation, make sure you understand what the problem is asking for. You are typically given the coordinates of two points (x , y ) and (x , y ), and asked to find the straight-line distance between them. The two points could be located on a plane or in three-dimensional space. For simplicity, we will focus on points in a two-dimensional plane in this tutorial. Key tip: Memorize the coordinate format as (x,y). It will help you avoid confusion while dealing with multiple sets of coordinates. Step 2: Recall the Distance Formula The distance d between two points (x , y ) and (x , y ) can be calculated by applying the Pythagorean theorem to their differences: d = sqrt[(x - x ) + (y - y ) ]. This equation represents the hypotenuse of a right triangle formed by connecting the two points and the origin (0,0); the legs of this triangle correspond to the difference between the respective x-values and y-values of the points. Step 3: Identify the Coordinates Identify your first point (x , y ) and second point (x , y ) from the problem statement. These values may already be provided directly, or they might require extraction from more complicated expressions. Remember that order matters; ensure you distinguish which set of coordinates corresponds to (x , y ) and which one corresponds to (x , y ). Step 4: Calculate the Differences Subtract the corresponding components of your points: x = x - x y = y - y These results represent the horizontal and vertical distances between your two points when visualized on a graph. Keeping track of these variables separately makes the final computation easier. Step 5: Square Each Difference Square both results obtained in Step 4: ( x) , ( y) . Be careful during squaring since negative numbers become positive-this operation ensures that all distances contribute positively to the overall result regardless of direction. Step 6: Sum Up Squared Differences Add the squares together: D = ( x) + ( y) . At this stage, you have essentially created the base of the imaginary right triangle mentioned earlier, preparing to calculate its hypotenuse length. Step 7: Compute the Square Root Now take the square root of the sum computed in Step 6: d = sqrt[D] = sqrt[( x) + ( y) ]. This action reverses the effect of squaring in Steps 5 and 6, yielding the actual straight-line distance between the original pair of points. Conclusion: Congratulations! You now know how to compute the distance between two points in a two-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system. Practice applying this method until it becomes intuitive, so you can tackle more intricate problems involving distance measurements. B i n g o b i n. Jimmy loved to explore the desert near his house. The level of them is usually used to determine its pH, which basically means the content of hydrogen ions in a particular volume. For example, hydrochloric acid, found in people's stomachs, can dissociate into a chloride anion and a free proton, and the property of the free proton is how it can digest food by corroding it. Title: How to Download Dropbox Files on Android Introduction: Dropbox is a popular cloud storage service that allows you to access your files from any device with an internet connection. If you're using an Android smartphone or tablet, you can download Dropbox files directly onto your device for offline viewing or editing. This tutorial will guide you through the process of downloading Dropbox files on Android step-by-step, ensuring clarity and practicality. Step 1: Install Dropbox App Firstly, ensure you have installed the official Dropbox app from Google Play Store on your Android device. Open the Play Store, search for 'Dropbox', then tap 'Install'. The installation may take a few moments depending upon your network speed. Installing the Dropbox app grants easy access to your account without requiring a web browser. Key Tip: Make sure your device has enough available space before installing new apps. Step 2: Sign Up / Log In Launch the newly installed Dropbox app. You now need to sign up if you don't already have a Dropbox account, or log in using your existing credentials (email address and password). Key Guideline: Keep your login information safe; avoid sharing it with others. Step 3: Navigate to Desired File Once logged in, you'll see all your folders and files stored in Dropbox. Browse through them until you find the file(s) you wish to download. Tap on the relevant folder or filename to open it. Step 4: Initiate Download After opening the required file, look at the top right corner of the screen where three vertical dots represent the 'More Options' menu. Tapping this button opens several choices including 'Make Available Offline'. Clicking this option initiates the download process. Key Terminology: A file marked as 'Available Offline' means it gets saved locally onto your Android device, enabling you to view/edit it even when no internet connection is present. Step 5: Monitor Progress Upon tapping 'Make Available Offline', a progress bar appears showing the status of the download. Wait till the entire operation finishes successfully. Larger files might take longer due to their size. Step 6: Verify Downloaded File To confirm whether the download was successful, navigate back to the main page of the Dropbox app. Underneath the name of each offline-available file, there should be a green tick mark indicating its availability offline. Also, check your phone's internal memory or SD card - the downloaded file must exist there too! Additional Tips: * To save data usage, connect to WiFi while downloading large files. * Regularly sync your Dropbox files to keep everything updated across devices. * Organize frequently accessed items into specific folders for quick retrieval later. Conclusion: With these simple steps, you can effortlessly download Dropbox files on your Android device anytime, anywhere. Whether you want to read documents during commute, watch videos offline, or edit photos without an active internet connection, having files synced via Dropbox ensures seamless productivity regardless of location or circumstances. [the wolf is asleep on the ground after having eaten the goats.] Joel Falconer wrote a great article on how to use your ear to detect intervals with song associations. In this article we'll look at how you come up with those intervals in the first place. Every few weeks, we revisit some of our reader's favorite posts from throughout the history of the site. This tutorial was first published in March of 2010. An interval is the distance between two notes, with the first note counted as 1. From C up to E is a major 3rd. From D to up G is a perfect 4th, and so on. There are two parts to an interval: a modifier and a number. First let's see how to figure out the number of an interval. You find the number by counting up the letters from your first note to your last. So let's say we wanted to find the number of the interval from C to A. Starting on C (counted as 1), we count up six letters (C D E F G A) to get to A, making C up to A an interval of a 6th. Sharps and flats are not used when figuring out the number of an interval, only the distance between the letters. So if we wanted to go from Db to G we ignore the flat and count the letters. Starting with D we have D E F G. Four letters, making Db up to G an interval of a 4th (we'll see what kind of 4th further down in the tutorial). The name for an interval of 1 is unison. The name for an interval of 8 is octave. So two identical Cs played at the same time are considered in unison. A C played with the C above it is an octave. Here are the interval numbers based off of C, from unison up to 13th: Now that we know how to count the number of an interval, let's look at how to figure out it's modifier. There are 5 possible modifiers of an interval: perfect, major, minor, diminished and augmented. Perfect intervals are used for unisons, 4ths, 5ths, and octaves. The best way to begin learning how to derive the perfect intervals is to think of the first note in the context of it's related major scale. For example if your first note was Eb and you wanted to go up a perfect 4th, you would start on Eb and count up the notes in the Eb major scale, Eb F G Ab. A P4 above Eb is Ab. Deriving a perfect 5th is the same process. Let's say we're starting on D. Count up 5 notes (with D as 1) in the D major scale and you land on A. Major and Minor Intervals The major and minor modifiers refer to intervals of a 2nd, 3rd, 6th and 7th. Again using your first note as if it is the 1 of a major scale, the major intervals are those that you'll find by going up a 2nd, 3rd, 6th or 7th above that note. In other words C to E is a major third. Ab to F is a major 6th. To change an interval from major to minor, you start with the major interval and then lower it by one half step. C to Db is a minor 2nd, C to Eb is a minor 3rd, C to Ab is a minor 6th, and C to Bb is a minor 7th. Diminished and Augmented Intervals A diminished interval (marked with a little circle ) is a half step lower than a minor or perfect interval. In real usage this usually only refers to a diminished 5th, in other words taking a Perfect 5th down a half step such as from C to Gb. An augmented interval (notated with a +) is the opposite of diminished. You augment an interval by raising a major or perfect interval by half a step. An augmented 4th would be C to F#, an augmented 6th is C to A#. The most common augmented intervals are the 4th and 5th. So remembering our interval of a 4th from earlier of Db to G, we can now see that it is an augmented 4th. How do I know that? Because a perfect 4th above Db is Gb (the 4th note up in the key of Db major). By raising the Gb up to a G we take it from Perfect up to Augmented. Also note that the diminished 5th and augmented 4th are more commonly referred to as the interval of a tritone. Let's now look at the common intervals from anyone note up to any other note, using C and E as our example starting pitches. Make sure you are perfectly clear on every one of these intervals before moving on. Inversions (Intervals Going Down) So far we've looked at how to figure out an interval from one note up to another, but how about from one note down to another? One of the easiest ways to do this is to "invert" the interval you already know. Moving in towards the center: an 2nd up becomes a 7th down, a 3rd up becomes a 6th down, and a 4th up becomes a 5th down. Of course the reverse is also true: a 5th up becomes a 4th down, a 6th up becomes a 3rd down and a 7th up becomes a 2nd down. A Major interval going up becomes a minor going down, and a minor interval going up becomes a major interval going down. Augmented intervals become diminished and vice versa. Perfect intervals remain perfect in either direction. OK so what does that all mean? In other words, going up from C to Bb is a minor 7th. To figure out the interval from C down to Bb, we switch minor to major and invert the 7th to a 2nd. Going C down to Bb is thus a major 2nd. As another example, Ab up to Db is a perfect 4th. To figure out Ab down to Db we leave the modifier of perfect as is, then invert the 4th to a 5th. We can then figure out that while Ab up to Db is a perfect 4th, Ab down to Db is a perfect 5th. Use these two charts as a reference: |Going up||Going down| |Going up||Going down| Finally, it's worth pointing out that although C to Ab (a minor 6th) and C to G# (an augmented 5th) may "sound" exactly the same, they should not be considered as identical. Their usage depends entirely on context. For now just remember that your number (2nd, 3rd, etc) comes from the number of letters from your first to your second note, and your modifier (major, minor, diminished, etc) comes from the actual distance between those two notes in relation to the major scale. I highly recommend you visit Joel's tutorial to begin to familiarize yourself with what the different intervals sound like, not just what they look like written out. Understanding intervals is a basic but crucial step in your development as a musician. With a firm grasp of intervals under your belt you'll be able to handle more advanced and rewarding topics such as chord tensions and counterpoint. Although memorizing all of the intervals from any note to any other note may seem tedious and boring, the long term rewards of doing so are immeasurable. Got a tip learning intervals or a question about how they're derived? Leave a comment and open up the discussion. Think of a star at a distance of 100 light years from us on Earth. [Ross was handing something back to Brian] Light Curves and What They Can Tell Us A light curve is a graph which shows the brightness of an object over a period of time. In the study of objects which change their brightness over time, such as novae, stars, the light curve is a simple but valuable tool to a scientist. A sample light curve is shown in Figure 1. The light curve is the graph, or plot, generated from the following data: |April 21 ||9.2 ||June 20 ||8.7 |April 27 ||9.3 ||June 26 ||8.3 |May 3 ||9.7 ||July 2 ||8.6 |May 9 ||9.9 ||July 8 ||9.1 |May 15 ||9.6 ||July 14 ||9.1 |May 21 ||9.8 ||July 20 ||9.2 |May 27 ||9.9 ||July 26 ||9.5 |June 2 ||9.7 ||Aug 1 ||9.9 |June 8 ||9.1 ||Aug 7 ||9.7 |June 14 ||8.8 ||Aug 13 ||9.7 The plot shows the brightness of a certain astronomical object viewed through a telescope every 6 days over the course of a few months. This gives us a light curve of the object we have measured. But light curves can be generated for any measure of brightness which is repeated over and over in time. So, if I measured the number of X-rays being emitted by a star during every second for an hour, I could generate a light curve from Astronomers call these plots light curves because it is usually some part of the spectrum that we measure as a function of time and use to help us understand our universe. How do we use light curves to tell us something useful? The record of changes in brightness that a light curve provides can help astronomers understand processes at work within the star (or stellar system) and identify specific categories (or classes) of stellar events. For example, once a light curve has been made for a certain stellar object, it can be compared to standard light curves to help identify the type of object being studied. If the light curve you measured looked like Figure 2, then you could identify your object as an eclipsing We can also tell from this light curve that it takes 10 days for one of the stars in the binary to orbit completely around the other. Astronomers would say "the orbital period of the binary system is 10 days." If the light curve looked like Figure 3, scientists would know that this signals the death of a star by a massive explosion called a supernova! It's a little baby just like you. The grass, the water, and the rocks all had blue ink on them. Fred laughed and thought it was funny. Now, everything was blue and pretty just like the ink. Who knew that a simple childhood game could help your kindergartener master math? But while it may never occur to your child that he's doing anything more than playing, counting games are a great way to get your kindergartener engaged in math. Take one-to-one correspondence, an important skill that is mastered in kindergarten. In a nutshell, one-to-one correspondence means children are able to count a group of objects, while touching each object, one at a time. (Kind of like that one potato game...) While this concept might seem obvious, it's more advanced than just counting out loud, which is called "rote counting" because it takes things to the next level-- associating spoken numbers with real objects. Give your child some hands-on practice with what teachers call good 'number sense', with this easy activity. What You Need: - 11 paper cups - 55 small objects (like cotton balls, pennies, beans, pasta, jelly beans, screws, or anything else that's fun to touch) What You Do: - After you've decided on the counting objects, get 11 medium-sized paper cups. Write the numbers 0 through 10 on each cup. Then mix up the cups and give them to your child. Ask your child to place the cups in order from smallest to biggest. - Give your child a collection of 55 objects. Tell her that the point of the game is to look at the number on each cup and put in the matching amount of objects. Be sure to remind your child to count out loud as she places each object in the cup. This helps to reinforce her counting skills and lets you assess her ability to count with one-to-one correspondence. - When your child is finished, empty the cups one at a time and have her check that the number of objects matches the number on the cup. - As your child develops accuracy and fluency working with 0 to 10, increase the numbers by increments of five, eventually working up to 30. It's not necessary to begin at 0 each time. Start in a place that's comfortable, and go as high as she can. As she gets better, pull the cups out in random order. See if she can do the game without the cups ascending. It makes things much trickier! All done? No need to throw the materials away. Keep a "counting box" in the house with various items that are safe for your child to count independently, and a set of cups with numbers. When you're busy making dinner, and your child is whining for something to do, you can say, "Get out the counting box!" Kids will never tire of this activity if you keep a colorful selection of things to count, so that each time your child opens the box, there's something new to explore. One day she saw something extra special. It was a screen! Sarah was very curious. She wanted to know what it was. So, Sarah pressed a button and the screen twisted. It showed different shapes and animals. Sarah was delighted! She smiled and laughed with joy. But then something happened. The screen seemed to get stuck and it just kept twisting. Sarah was a little bit scared. But then she remembered something her mommy told her. She needed to be patient. So, Sarah took a deep breath and waited. Sure enough, after a few minutes, the screen stopped twisting. She was so relieved! She clapped her hands and danced around the room. Sarah was amazed by the screen. She knew she learned something special that day and she would always be patient when her toys got stuck. The end. You can play with it, but it's not yours. Title: How to Deal with Early Periods Early periods can be an unexpected and sometimes stressful experience for young girls. However, understanding what is happening and learning how to manage early periods can help alleviate any anxiety or discomfort. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to deal with early periods, including in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines. **Step 1: Understanding Early Periods** An early period refers to menstruation that occurs before the age of 9 years old. The average age for a girl's first period (menarche) is around 12 years old, although this can vary widely from one individual to another. Menstruation is part of the reproductive cycle where the uterus sheds its lining if pregnancy does not occur. Hormonal changes during puberty trigger menstruation. *Why this step matters:* Knowing what constitutes an 'early' period sets realistic expectations and reduces unnecessary worry. It also encourages open communication about physical changes between parents/guardians and their daughters. **Step 2: Recognizing the Signs** Before actual menstruation begins, there are several signs that may indicate its imminence. These include: - Breast development - Pubic hair growth - Body odor changes - Acne - Mood swings - Bloating Recognizing these symptoms can prepare both parent and child for the upcoming event. *Key Tip*: Encourage regular check-ups with pediatricians or family doctors to monitor overall health and discuss any concerns related to puberty. **Step 3: Preparing for the First Period** Prepare a 'period kit' containing sanitary napkins or tampons, clean underwear, wipes, and a plastic bag for disposal. Keep this kit readily available at home, school, or anywhere else your daughter spends significant time. *Guideline*: Choose hypoallergenic products to minimize risk of irritation or allergies. **Step 4: Managing the First Period** When the first period arrives, assist your daughter in using the sanitary product of her choice comfortably. Explain how often they need changing based on flow volume. Teach proper hygiene practices such as washing hands before and after changing pads/tampons. *Tips*: - Use panty liners for lighter days. - Change pads/tampons every four hours to prevent leakage and infection. **Step 5: Coping With Emotional Changes** Puberty brings emotional fluctuations due to hormone shifts. Girls might feel self-conscious, anxious, or moody. Reassure them that these feelings are normal and offer support. *Key Guideline*: Practice empathy and patience; remember that everyone experiences puberty differently. **Step 6: Seeking Medical Attention** If your daughter experiences unusually heavy bleeding, severe cramps, dizziness, or fainting, seek medical attention immediately. While some discomfort is common during periods, excessive pain could signal underlying conditions like endometriosis or polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Remember, dealing with early periods requires more than just managing physiological aspects - it involves supporting emotional wellbeing too. By following this guide, you'll equip yourself and your daughter with the necessary knowledge and tools to handle early periods effectively and confidently. What else do we see in here? Essen (; Low Franconian and Ripuarian: "Esse", Latin: "Assindia") is a German city in the state of North Rhine-Westphalia. It is on the Ruhr River and it is near Cologne, Duisburg and Bochum. 585,000 people live in Essen. Together with Dortmund, Essen is the biggest city of the Ruhr area with its 5,000,000 inhabitants. Concept A: Absolute Zero. The lowest possible temp is around -273degC. It is labeled 0K on the Kelvin scale. K = degC + 273 degC = K - 273 Gas volumes are proportional to K temps. A temp change from 20K to 40K will double the volume. A temp change from 20degC to 40degC does not. (It will increase the volume by 313K / 293K.) ALL TEMPS USED IN GAS LAWS MUST BE IN KELVINS. Concept B: Standard Temperature and Pressure. (STP) Solids and liquids have a defined volume. Gases have a defined volume if the temperature and pressure are defined. Standard Temperature = 0degC = 273K Standard Pressures = 1 atmosphere (atm) = 760mmHg = 760torr = 101.3kPa = 14.7psi (pounds per square inch) = 29.9inHg. You'll probably have to convert between these units. Equation 1: The combined gas laws. P2V2 / T2 = P1V1 / T1 Temps have to be in Kelvins. Pressures have to have the same units, but it doesn't matter which one. Volumes have to have the same units, but it doesn't matter which one. This equation is used to figure out a new value when a fixed amount of gas undergoes a change in conditions of PV or T. Typically a volume of gas will be collected at lab conditions (say 25degC and 740.mmHg) and you will be asked to calculate the volume collected at STP. V2 = V1P1T2 / P2T1. Equation 2: Ideal Gas Law PV = nRT. This equation is used to calculate one value when the other 3 are known. T must be in Kelvins. P and V must have the same units as R. (or R the same units as P and V) n measures moles. R is a constant and can be calculated based on the units given in the problem by substituting standard conditions and volume (22.4L or equivalent) for 1 mole of a gas. R = (PV / nT) R = (1atm)(22.4L) /[(1mole)(273K)] = 0.0821Latm/moleK R = (760torr)(22.4dm^3) / [(1mole)(273K)] = 62.4torrdm^3/moleK etc. By substituting m/M (mass/molarmass) for n, a couple other interesting equations show up. PV = mRT / M P = mRT / MV PM / RT = m/V = D For molar mass PV = mRT / M MPV = mRT M = mRT / PV Gas law 3: Graham's Law v1 / v2 = sqrt(M2 / M1) v (lower case) stands for velocity not Volume (upper case V). M stands for molar mass. This equation applies to two samples of different gases at the same temp and pressure. If the velocity of one gas is known, the velocity of the other can be calculated. Alternatively, the "relative rates" of gas1 to gas2 can be calculated. Or gas1 is how many times faster than gas2? In the latter two examples it's that ratio that's important not a specific value for v1 or v2. Note that the subscripts are criss-crossed in the equation. It's possible you will also have to calculate the volume of a dry gas, but that requires an explanation of open and closed manometers. You may also have to understand how to do a calculation involving a real gas. The equation contains corrections that compensate for the assumptions made about ideal gases: 1 that particles are point masses, (have mass but occupy not space.) 2 that collisions are completely elastic (no energy lost.) See it and a description of the rest of the laws and some examples at Yes it does sound like sand, but it doesn't have any sand in it. What happened to the boy and the girl in the story? Title: How to Decorate a School Locker for a Birthday Introduction: Decorating a school locker for a friend's birthday is an exciting way to add some personalized flair and show your appreciation. With creativity, colorful materials, and careful planning, you can transform an ordinary locker into a festive celebration space that will make your friend feel special. This article provides a comprehensive guide on decorating a school locker for a birthday with clear steps, helpful tips, and crucial considerations. Section 1: Gathering Necessary Materials Before starting any project, it's essential to gather all necessary supplies. Here are recommended items for this task: - Wrapping paper or contact paper (to cover the interior) - Tape - Scissors - Ribbons and bows - Photographs or posters - Magnets or adhesive strips (for securing photos or posters) - Balloons - Streamers - Fake flowers or garlands - Stickers or decals - Treats or small gifts (optional) Key Tip: Make sure the items you choose do not damage the locker surface or violate school policies regarding locker decoration. Always remove decorations promptly after the birthday celebration to avoid potential issues. Section 2: Cleaning and Preparing the Space Begin by cleaning out the locker and removing any unnecessary clutter. A clean slate allows more room for creative expression and makes it easier to attach decorations securely. Additionally, ensure the area around the locker is free from trash and debris. Section 3: Covering the Interior Surfaces Using wrapping paper or contact paper, line the inside surfaces of the locker. Measure and cut pieces according to the locker dimensions. Attach them using tape along the edges. This creates a cohesive backdrop and adds visual appeal. Choose colors and patterns that match your theme or reflect your friend's preferences. Section 4: Adding Personalization Incorporate photographs, posters, stickers, or decals related to your friend's interests, hobbies, or favorite things. Secure these elements with magnets or adhesive strips, ensuring they don't leave residue behind. By adding personal touches, you create a meaningful display that shows thoughtfulness and care. Section 5: Creating Visual Interest Use balloons, streamers, fake flowers, and garlands to build height, texture, and movement within the locker. Fill the bottom with balloon bouquets tied together with ribbon; layer streamers along walls or across the top shelf; weave garlands through open spaces for added detail. These components contribute to creating a dynamic, eye-catching arrangement. Key Guideline: Be mindful of fire safety regulations when incorporating tissue paper products like streamers and balloons. Refrain from blocking access to emergency exits or obstructing visibility near doors or windows. Section 6: Including Small Gifts or Treats If permitted by school policy, surprise your friend with small presents or treats tucked among the decorations. Place these items strategically throughout the locker so they remain hidden until discovered during the birthday celebration. Consider packing snacks, stationery, trinkets, or other affordable tokens of affection. Conclusion: Transforming a school locker into a celebratory space requires attention to detail, creativity, and careful selection of appropriate materials. Following this thorough guide ensures you accomplish the task efficiently while achieving impressive results. Your effort and thoughtfulness will undoubtedly bring joy and excitement to your friend's birthday! Sometimes I read and sometimes she'll read. Title: How to Forward Emails from Your School Email Program Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of forwarding emails from your school email program. This can be useful if you want to have all your emails sent to one central location or if you need someone else to manage your school emails. The steps may vary slightly depending on the specific email program used by your school, but this general guide should help you get started. Step 1: Log into Your School Email Account Before you begin forwarding emails, you must first log into your school email account using your username and password. Make sure that you are accessing the correct webpage for your school's email system. Once logged in, take note of any custom settings or options available as they might affect how you set up email forwarding. Key tip: Bookmark the login page for easy future reference. Step 2: Locate the Settings Menu Once logged in, look for an icon or link typically labeled as 'Settings', 'Options', 'Gear Icon,' or something similar. Click on it to open the settings menu. If you cannot find such an option within the main interface, check the top-right or bottom-left corners of the screen. Guideline: Some schools may have different layouts; therefore, familiarize yourself with the overall design so you know where to locate essential features like the settings menu. Step 3: Access the Mail (or Email) Settings Section In the settings menu, search for a section called 'Mail' (also known as 'Email'), 'Accounts,' or another term related to managing your email preferences. This is usually located under the primary headings in the settings menu, often near the top. Step 4: Find the Email Forwarding Option Within the mail settings section, there should be a subsection dedicated specifically to email forwarding. Look for keywords like 'Forwarding,' 'POP,' or 'IMAP.' These terms refer to protocols that allow incoming messages to be rerouted to other email accounts. Select the appropriate option to proceed. Key tip: When enabling email forwarding, make sure to leave a copy of the message in the original inbox unless you don't wish to keep records of these emails at the source. Step 5: Enter the Destination Address You will now see a field asking for the destination address - the email account where you would like your school emails forwarded. Type in the complete email address carefully to avoid errors during setup. After entering the recipient email address, click 'Save Changes' or its equivalent button. A confirmation message may appear indicating successful activation of email forwarding. Step 6: Test the Email Forwarding Functionality To ensure everything works correctly, send a test email from another personal email account to your school email address. Wait a few moments for the email to arrive at the designated forwarding address. Check spam folders too, just in case. If the test email arrives without issues, then congratulations! You have successfully set up email forwarding for your school email account. Additional Tips: * Always remember to periodically review your school email account even when relying heavily on forwarding, as some important communications could potentially end up being filtered incorrectly and thus missed. * Keep track of your school email account's storage limits since having thousands of emails stored there due to unmanaged forwarding could cause problems over time. Regular maintenance and cleanup are recommended practices. * Disable email forwarding once no longer needed. Leaving it enabled exposes sensitive information contained within those emails to potential security risks. Based on an idea by Stacie Kreitman, Kilmer Middle School, Falls Church, VA This exercise allows students to calculate the abundance of elements in different substances.Materials and Preparation: Each element is represented by a different food found in a kitchen. Below are suggested items for different elements. Different colored candy sprinkles also work well. Each item should be approximately the same size. |H - White Rice||He - Green Split Peas||O - Brown Rice| |C - Black Beans||Fe - Red Lentils||N - Brown Lentils| |Ar - Blue Sprinkles||Si - Pearl Barley||Mg - Wild Rice| Prepare mixtures of these items according to the following recipes. Note that these abundances are by number, not weight. So measure using dry measure, not a scale. Below are listed the percentages, and the amount of each element for a total of 10 oz for each substance (Note that the percentages may not add to 100% due to our excluding less significant elements.) In general, 1/8 cup represents 10% of the total amount, and 1 teaspoon represents 1% of the total amount. Place each into a separate jar or bottle. (11 oz. plastic water bottles work well). Cap all the jars/bottles. Seal some of them using superglue, but leave others that can be opened. Also give them a copy of the key as to what element each type of object represents. Have the students estimate the composition of the bottles by giving the fraction of hydrogen, fraction of helium, etc. Note that students with bottles that can be opened can directly sample the material, but those with sealed bottles must estimate visually. Now give the students the abundances for the different objects. Have the students determine what type of object their bottle represents. [ROM continues to "drink" from his cup] Title: Have the Most Fun Day Ever as a Tween Introduction: As a tween (ages 10-12), you are at an exciting stage where you can enjoy activities that cater to both kids and teenagers. This tutorial will guide you through planning and experiencing the most fun day ever! With these steps, you'll learn how to create meaningful memories while fostering independence and responsibility. **Step 1: Brainstorm Activities** Key Tip: Think about your interests and passions when brainstorming activities. Consider inviting friends along or spending quality time with family members. * Rationale*: By focusing on what you love, you increase the likelihood of having a blast throughout the day. Including others can also add to the excitement and help strengthen relationships. **Step 2: Create a Schedule** Guideline: Allocate specific times for each activity, ensuring there is enough buffer between them to account for travel or unexpected events. * Rationale*: A well-planned schedule ensures smooth transitions from one activity to another without feeling rushed or overwhelmed. It instills good time management skills necessary for future endeavors. **Step 3: Gather Supplies** Key Tip: Make a list of all required items for each activity ahead of time. Check off each item as you pack them to avoid forgetting anything important. * Rationale*: Preparation leads to less stress during execution. Having everything you need readily available allows you to focus more on enjoying yourself rather than scrambling for missing supplies. **Step 4: Seek Parental Approval** Guideline: Share your plan with your parents or guardians, addressing any concerns they might have regarding safety, supervision, or logistics. * Rationale*: Adult involvement provides reassurance and guidance. Their expertise and resources may enhance your experience and ensure a secure environment. **Step 5: Execute Your Plan** Key Tips: Stay positive, be adaptable, and communicate openly with those involved in your day. Remember, flexibility is crucial since things may not always go according to plan. * Rationale*: Maintaining a positive attitude encourages enthusiasm and motivation throughout the day. Adapting to changes showcases resilience, an essential life skill. Open communication fosters stronger connections and promotes problem-solving. **Step 6: Reflect on Your Experience** Guideline: At the end of the day, take some time to reflect on what went well and areas for improvement. Document your thoughts in a journal or share them with someone close to you. * Rationale*: Reflection enhances self-awareness and growth. Identifying successes and challenges helps refine future plans and increases overall satisfaction. By following these six steps, you're setting yourself up for an unforgettable day filled with laughter, learning, and lasting memories. Happy adventuring! The book with the words for Puff. [a little louder and raspier; 1 sec] A radical is also in simplest form when the radicand is not a fraction. Example 1. 33, for example, has no square factors. Its factors are 3 11, neither of which is a square number. Therefore, is in its simplest form. Example 2. Extracting the square root. 18 has the square factor 9. 18 = 9 2. Therefore, is not in its simplest form. We have, We may now extract, or take out, the square root of 9: = = 3. is now simplified. The radicand no longer has any square factors. The justification for taking out the square root of 9, is this theorem: The square root of a product (We will prove that when we come to rational exponents, Lesson 29. As for , then, it is equal to the square root of 9 times the square root of 2, which is irrational. 3. Example 3 Simplify . Solution. = = 5. 75 has the square factor 25. And the square root of 25 times 3 is now simplified. Example 4. Simplify . Solution. We have to factor 42 and see if it has any square factors. We can begin the factoring in any way. For example, 42 = 6 7 We can continue to factor 6 as 2 3, but we cannot continue to factor 7, because 7 is a prime number (Lesson 32 of Arithmetic). Therefore, 42 = 2 3 7 We now see that 42 has no square factors -- because no factor is repeated. Compare Example 1 and Problem 2 of the previous Lesson. therefore is in its simplest form. Example 5. Simplify . Solution. We must look for square factors, which will be factors that are repeated. 180 = 2 90 = 2 2 45 = 2 2 9 5 = 2 2 3 3 5 = 2 3 = 6. Problem 1. Title: How to Delete a Private Slack Channel on an Android Device Slack is a popular communication platform that allows users to create channels for different topics or teams within their organization. As a user, you may find yourself needing to delete a private channel at some point. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting a private Slack channel using your Android device. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** 1. Make sure you are the creator or have been granted appropriate permissions to delete the channel. Only the creators and admins can delete a channel. 2. Deletion is permanent; there's no way to recover deleted messages or files once the action has been confirmed. 3. Double-check before confirming deletion as all data, including messages, files, integrations, and settings, will be removed permanently. **Step 1: Open the Slack app on your Android device.** Start by finding and tapping on the Slack icon on your home screen or app drawer. Once open, log into your account if necessary. **Step 2: Navigate to the private channel you want to delete.** From the Slack main screen, select the workspace where the private channel resides. You might need to swipe left or right along the bottom navigation bar to access other workspaces. Then, tap on 'Channels' from the menu followed by 'More...' (three vertical dots) next to the private channel you wish to remove. **Step 3: Tap 'Additional options.'** This option expands the contextual menu providing further actions related to the selected private channel. **Step 4: Select 'Settings.'** Tapping this option takes you to the channel settings page where various customization options are available. **Step 5: Scroll down and choose 'Advanced settings.'** On the channel settings page, scroll towards the end until you see the heading labeled 'Advanced settings'. Click on it. **Step 6: Tap 'Delete this channel.'** Under advanced settings, you should see several options listed vertically, one being 'Delete this channel'. Upon clicking this button, two prompts appear asking for confirmation about the deletion action. **Step 7: Confirm deletion twice.** The first prompt warns about the irreversible nature of deletion while the second confirms the final removal. Carefully read both warnings ensuring accuracy especially with respect to naming the correct channel intended for deletion. After verifying, tap 'Yes, delete it' to proceed. Once completed, the specified private channel gets deleted permanently along with its entire contents, history, members, and associated information. Always ensure due diligence prior to executing such critical tasks since recovered data after deletion is impossible within the application itself. Dad does Mary's class come with yours? Once there was a boy named Bob. Bob went to school every day and worked really hard. He was the most successful pupil at the school, and the other children looked up to him. One day, Bob was walking down the hallway and heard a loud yell coming from the principal's office. He stopped and listened, and heard the principal yelling at another boy. Bob was worried and went to investigate. He opened the office door and saw the principal yelling at little Johnny. Johnny looked scared and sad, like he was going to cry. Bob stepped up and said in a loud, clear voice. "Stop yelling at him! What did he do?" The principal looked surprised, and then he said, "Johnny was late to school three times this week. I'm trying to teach him a lesson." Bob said, "You should not yell at him. That's not how you teach someone. Have patience and talk to him kindly." The principal was surprised by his wisdom, and agreed. The next day, Bob saw Johnny in the hallway and he was smiling. He had listened to what Bob said and was not late to school anymore. Johnny was really successful and Bob was happy that he was able to help. Do you know how to put these things together? Okay now put him a king give him a king Mark. And then you're going to be six. Putting the jar away] During apartheid, people were divided into four racial groups and separated by law. The system was used to deny many basic rights to non-White people, mainly Black people who lived in South Africa. The law allowed white people to be in certain areas. Black people had to carry special passes or have permission to travel outside their designated area, or work in particular areas reserved for Whites. The government separated mixed communities and forcibly moved many black people off their land (From 1960 to 1983 the Bantustan Policy was enforced to forcibly move South African blacks out of the areas designated for 'whites'). Many other laws were made, for example: interracial marriage was outlawed; Black people could not own land in White areas or vote. Emergent Literacy Design: to the Doctor By: Mari Manning Rationale: This lesson will help children identify /o/ They will learn to recognize /o/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation and a symbol. They will then practice finding /o/ in written words. They will use a meaningful representation to help them read words that contain /o/. Since short vowels are tough phonemes for young children to identify, it is important to make sure children learn phonemes. Children need to be exposed to different phonemes in order to read and spell, and they must recognize the spoken context before they can recognize them in the written context. This lesson will give children exposure needed to identify /o/ in the spoken and written contexts. - Chart with "Ollie the octopus opted out of the opposites contest with the ostrich in October" - Primary paper and pencils - Set of cards with /o/ pictures and words on them (dog, stop, octopus, ostrich, mop, frog, cop, sock, hot, pot, shop, knob, clock, lock, and rod) for tongue twister challenge - Frog Cops by Scholastic - Drawing paper and crayons - Chalk and chalkboard - Picture page with hat, dog, bed, stop sign, octopus, mop, frog, dock, bus, ship, king, rock, music notes (song), ball, and truck. - Introduce the lesson by explaining that it is hard to recognize short vowels in our spoken and written language. "The tricky part of learning to recognize these vowels is learning which letters stand for which mouth moves we make as we say words. Today we are going to work on spotting the mouth move /o/. It may be hard for you to spot /o/ in spoken and written words at first, but as we practice it and get to know it well, we will be able to spot /o/ in both written and spoken words." - Ask students: "Have you ever been to the doctor and had to open your mouth and say /o/ as he looked down your throat? Well, that is the mouth move we will be looking for today. Let's pretend to be at the doctor and open our mouths and say /o/ as he looks down our throats (have class open mouths and say /o/). We say /o/ to open our throats so the doctor can look down them and see any problems. Let's open our throats for the doctor: /o/." - Let's try a tongue twister (on chart). "Ollie the octopus opted out of the opposites contest with the ostrich in October." Everyone say it three times together (class says it together). Now let's say it again, and this time, let's stretch the /o/ at the beginning of the words. "Ooollie the oooctopus ooopted ooout ooof the ooopposites contest with the ooostrich in Oooctober." Try it again, and this time break it off the word: "/o/ llie the /o/ ctopus /o/ pted /o/ ut /o/ f the /o/ pposites contest with the /o/ tter in /o/ ctober." - Have students take out primary paper and pencil. Tell students: "We can use the letter o to spell /o/. Let's write it first. Start at the fence line. Draw half a circle around to the sidewalk and another half circle back around to the fence line. Do this without lifting your pencil. When you finish, you should have a circle. I want to see After I put a smiley face on your paper, I want you to make 9 more just like it. When you see the letter o all by itself in a word, that's the signal to say /o/ like we've practiced. When you make your 9 more o's, I want to hear you say /o/ as you are making them." - Have children stand up in a circle and get ready to play the tongue twister challenge. Have ready-made cards with /o/ words and pictures on them, either beginning with /o/ or have /o/ in the middle. The first child takes the top card and looks at the picture to determine the word (example: dog). The second child says the first child's word and adds a second word to it that has /o/ in it (example: dog, stop). Continue with as many children as are participating. After each new word, the entire class chants the chain of words thus far. Encourage the children to see how quickly they can say or chant the words. Children can then write down tongue twisters of their own using /o/. The tongue twisters can be displayed in the hall or in the - Read Frog Cops and discuss the story. Read the story again and have children get with a partner and shine their doctor lights down their partner's throats while the partner opens his or her mouth to form /o/ each time they hear a word with /o/ (children can take turns). Have a child, or yourself, list those words on the board. Then have the children draw a frog and write a short message about it using invented spelling. These too can be displayed in the classroom or in the hallway. - For assessment, pass out a picture page that contains several rows of pictures of things with /o/ and things without /o/. Help children name each picture just to make sure they know what it is. Ask them to draw a box around each picture whose name has Gaydos, Nora. Frog Cops. New York: Scholastic. 2001. Leet (sometimes written as "1337" or "l33t"), also known as eleet or leet-speak, is another alphabet for the English language that is used mostly on the internet. It uses various combinations of ASCII characters to replace Latinate letters. For example, leet spellings of the word "leet" include "1337" and "l33t"; "eleet" may be spelled "31337" or "3l33t". It is used on the internet in forums, chat rooms and online games. Or I'll keep it brother, or I'll keep it little BA' brother, or I'll keep it little baby, or I'll keep it whatever. [points to the crayon marks on the paper] solve one problem, and then must find the other person in the room who has their "Other Half" i.e. a different problem with the same This activity can be quick or long depending upon how hard you make the problems. It gets folks talking math with each other. Create a set of use them repeatedly as warm-ups, or quick fillers, days when time is not plentiful. IT LOOKS LIKE: - Write out an answer guide with pairs of problems; each pair should have two different problems that both end with the same answer. click HERE to see - Next, make up actual cards with these problems on them. Each card should have its own circled identification number at the top. Keep these cards together by to see and example - Before your students arrive, write 1st place, 2nd place, 3rd place etc. on the board and place a piece of tape underneath each place. (This will get students interested as soon as they enter.) - Begin by passing out scrap paper to each student and have them write their name on it. - Pass the cards out making sure that matches do not go to kids sitting beside each other. (You may want to plan it so that you pass the harder questions to the brighter kids so that they are more challenged.) If you have an odd number of students, give one of the harder problems to the brightest student and tell her there is no match for it, she just has to get it correct. - Students copy down the problem and its circled identification number onto their scrap paper and then do it showing all work. - Next they must find their "Other Half" somewhere in the room. - When they find each other they quickly go to the board and tape their scrap papers face down under the 1st or 2nd or 3rd place etc. spots, in order. - When everyone is taped up, the teacher will check to see if the mathches on the board are correct. - We recommend letting at least half of the pairs win some sort of prize or bonus to keep the slower students trying. The first place pair can win a better prize or more bonus. Lions appear in heraldry more often than any other animal.
